mnemonics: Bubble Up, Select Out: Bubbling floats the largest element to the top (end) in each pass, while Selection finds the smallest element and swaps it to the front.
Sinking Bubbles, Selecting Champions: Imagine bubbles sinking the heaviest (largest) element down, while a selection process picks a champion (smallest) for the front position.
keyPoints: Selection Sort scans the unsorted part to find the minimum element and swaps it to the sorted front.
Bubble Sort repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order, 'bubbling' the largest value to the end.
Both are comparison-based, in-place sorting algorithms with an average and worst-case time complexity of O(n²).
commonMistakes: Confusing the inner loop conditions: Selection Sort finds the min index, Bubble Sort compares adjacent neighbors.
Assuming Bubble Sort is efficient due to its name; it is one of the least efficient sorting algorithms for large datasets.
Forgetting that both algorithms have a best-case time complexity of O(n²), unlike other algorithms like Insertion Sort which can be O(n) for nearly sorted data.
